content.text: Asyncio's networking layer is built on a two-layer abstraction that separates
, which owns the raw I/O channel; on top sits the
, which owns the application logic that interprets those bytes. Understanding both — and how they cooperate — unlocks the ability to build performant, production-grade async network code in Python.
pattern is asyncio's low-level networking API. It is a callback-based design inspired by the Twisted networking framework, and it predates the higher-level streams API (

and its subclasses) represents the underlying communication channel — a TCP socket, a UDP socket, a subprocess pipe, or an SSL connection. It knows how to write bytes, close the connection, and query connection metadata. It does
know anything about the meaning of those bytes.

and its subclasses) is a collection of callback methods that get called by the transport when interesting things happen: data arrived, the connection was made, the connection was lost. The protocol knows the application logic but does not directly control the socket.

These two objects are always created together and wired to each other. The event loop drives the transport; the transport calls back into the protocol; the protocol can call write methods on the transport.
Most Python async code uses the

API, which is simpler but adds overhead. The Transport/Protocol layer exists for situations where that overhead matters — high-throughput servers, custom protocols, wrapping non-standard I/O channels, or building protocol implementations that ship as libraries (like

Understanding this layer also reveals how asyncio actually works under the hood: the event loop is fundamentally a reactor that polls file descriptors and fires callbacks. Streams are just a coroutine-friendly wrapper over exactly that callback mechanism.
as the worker sitting next to the pipe.
The pipe (transport) knows how to push bytes in and pull bytes out. It does not care whether those bytes are HTTP, raw JSON, or a custom binary format.

The worker (protocol) knows what to do when bytes arrive. When the pipe has something, it taps the worker on the shoulder (calls a callback). When the worker wants to send something, it hands bytes back to the pipe.
The event loop is the supervisor that keeps polling the pipe to see if there is anything new.
The event loop registers file descriptors with the OS using
(depending on platform). When the OS reports that a socket is readable, the loop calls an internal read callback, which buffers the incoming data and then calls
. When the socket is writable after a queued write, the loop drains the write buffer. The protocol never touches the socket directly.

loop.create_connection() / loop.create_server()
        │
        ▼
   Transport created (wraps the OS socket)
        │
        ▼
   protocol.connection_made(transport) ← wired together here
        │
        ▼  [data flows in]
   protocol.data_received(data)         ← called by transport
        │
        ▼  [connection closes]
   protocol.connection_lost(exc)        ← called by transport
Send EOF on the write side (half-close)
True if close has been requested
(implemented by you, called by the transport):
Transport is ready; store the transport reference here
A chunk of bytes arrived (TCP only)
Remote side closed its write end
Write buffer is above the high-water mark
Write buffer has drained below low-water mark
The full interaction is shown below, along with flow control.---
Asyncio ships with several transport classes, each suited to a different kind of I/O:
— one-directional, read-only (e.g., a subprocess's stdout pipe). Methods:
— one-directional, write-only (e.g., a subprocess's stdin pipe). Methods:
— the most common case. Inherits both read and write transports. A TCP connection uses this.
— for UDP, where individual datagrams are discrete. Methods:
— wraps a child process, exposing its stdin as a
— for stream-oriented connections (TCP, SSL, pipes). Key callbacks:
, because each datagram carries a source address.
is the critical lifecycle hook. This is where
must be saved because every subsequent write goes through it.

async def main():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()

    # The factory is called once per accepted connection
    server = await loop.create_server(
        protocol_factory=EchoProtocol,
        host="127.0.0.1",
        port=8888,
    )

    async with server:
        print("Serving on 127.0.0.1:8888")
        await server.serve_forever()

asyncio.run(main())
that returns a new protocol instance — a class reference works because calling a class produces a new instance.

async def connect_client():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()

    transport, protocol = await loop.create_connection(
        protocol_factory=EchoProtocol,
        host="127.0.0.1",
        port=8888,
    )

    transport.write(b"Hello, server!\n")
    await asyncio.sleep(1)      # wait for the echo
    transport.close()

asyncio.run(connect_client())
returns both the transport and protocol objects. In most usage you interact with the transport to send data and rely on protocol callbacks for anything received.

Flow control is one of the most important and least understood parts of the transport/protocol API. When a slow consumer cannot read bytes as fast as a fast producer writes them, the write buffer grows without bound — eventually exhausting memory.
Asyncio solves this with a high-water / low-water mark on the transport's write buffer:

Buffer size:  0 ──────────── low-water ────────────── high-water ──── ∞
                                   │                        │
                                   │                        └─ pause_writing() called
                                   └──────────────────────────── resume_writing() called
When the write buffer exceeds the high-water mark, the transport calls
. The protocol should stop producing data. When the buffer drains below the low-water mark, the transport calls
implements internally — understanding it here makes the streams API less magical.
You can configure the water marks when creating the transport:

transport = await loop.create_connection(
    EchoProtocol,
    host="127.0.0.1",
    port=8888,
)
# After creation:
transport.set_write_buffer_limits(high=65536, low=16384)
UDP is connectionless, so its protocol is structurally different. Each received packet carries a source address, and writes go to an explicit destination.
— UDP errors like ICMP port-unreachable are not connection-fatal events; they arrive as notifications. The connection is never "lost" from the OS perspective because UDP has no connection to lose.
SSL is handled by the transport layer. An
and handles the TLS handshake transparently. The protocol sees exactly the same callbacks.

import ssl

ssl_context = ssl.create_default_context()
ssl_context.load_cert_chain("server.crt", "server.key")

server = await loop.create_server(
    EchoProtocol,
    host="0.0.0.0",
    port=8443,
    ssl=ssl_context,
)
(which uses the default context for clients). Inside
, useful for inspecting the negotiated cipher or certificate.
Asyncio can spawn child processes and communicate with them via the Transport/Protocol API:

class SubprocessProtocol(asyncio.SubprocessProtocol):

    def __init__(self, exit_future: asyncio.Future):
        self.exit_future = exit_future
        self.output = bytearray()

    def pipe_data_received(self, fd: int, data: bytes) -> None:
        # fd 1 = stdout, fd 2 = stderr
        if fd == 1:
            self.output.extend(data)

    def process_exited(self) -> None:
        self.exit_future.set_result(True)


async def run_subprocess():
    loop = asyncio.get_running_loop()
    exit_future = loop.create_future()

    transport, protocol = await loop.subprocess_exec(
        lambda: SubprocessProtocol(exit_future),
        "python3", "-c", "print('hello from child')",
        stdin=None,
    )

    await exit_future
    transport.close()
    print("Output:", bytes(protocol.output).decode())

asyncio.run(run_subprocess())
fires separately for each pipe; the protocol is responsible for routing by fd.
Real-world example: building a line-oriented protocol server
Many real-world protocols (SMTP, Redis, IRC, Memcached) are line-based: commands end with
call per logical line — a single call may deliver half a line, a full line, or multiple lines at once. A correct protocol implementation must buffer and parse.

This pattern — accumulate in a buffer, scan for a delimiter, extract complete frames — is the correct and universal approach. It cleanly handles both fragmented delivery and coalesced delivery regardless of network conditions.
Transport vs. Streams: when to use each
) wraps Transport/Protocol internally and exposes a coroutine-based interface via

The rule of thumb: use streams for application code and one-off scripts; use Transport/Protocol when building a library, wrapping a non-TCP channel, or when profiling shows the streams wrapper is a measurable bottleneck.
Forgetting to store the transport in
, the protocol cannot write responses. This is the most frequent beginner error.
TCP is a stream protocol. There is no message boundary guarantee. Half a JSON object, two JSON objects fused together, or any other combination may arrive in a single call. Always buffer and frame.
fires. Writing to it raises an error or silently drops data. Guard with
, a fast producer paired with a slow consumer will grow the write buffer until memory is exhausted. For any production server that sends substantial data, flow control is mandatory.
It is legal but almost always wrong. If connection validation logic needs to reject a client, do it by sending an error message first, then calling
Mixing blocking I/O inside protocol callbacks.
The callbacks run on the event loop thread. Any blocking call (a synchronous database call,
, a heavy CPU computation) blocks the entire event loop and freezes all other connections. Offload blocking work to a thread executor:
drops the write buffer and hard-closes the socket, which may cause data loss. Only use it when the remote peer is misbehaving and a clean close is impossible.
(rare but possible with custom event loops).
. Many production bugs stem from leaked resources (open file handles, semaphores, database connections) that are never released because
for any server that sends more than trivial amounts of data. The simplest correct implementation is a boolean flag that prevents additional writes until
time to log client addresses. This is the only reliable moment to capture the remote address — it may not be available after the connection closes.
Keep protocol state machines explicit. For complex protocols (HTTP, SMTP, WebSocket), maintain a
enum and transition it deliberately. Implicit state encoded in boolean flags becomes unmaintainable quickly.
Set a timeout for idle connections. The event loop provides
which can be reset on each
. Without timeouts, half-open connections accumulate indefinitely.
A single asyncio event loop on a modern server can handle tens of thousands of concurrent TCP connections, limited primarily by file descriptor limits (
) and memory. Each connection's protocol object is lightweight — typically a few hundred bytes — so connection count is not itself the bottleneck.
CPU work is done in callbacks
— parse a large JSON payload in
and the event loop stalls for all connections. Solution: offload with
or batch parse with a background task.
calls fills system memory. Solution: implement
— accumulation buffers that are never reset, state that references old data. Solution: clear buffers in
For extreme throughput requirements, replace the default event loop with
, which reimplements the event loop in Cython using
. It is a drop-in replacement that typically doubles network throughput:
For multi-core utilization, run one asyncio event loop per CPU core in separate processes (not threads — the GIL and
are per-thread), each binding to the same port with
. The kernel distributes incoming connections across processes.
— a production-grade event loop replacement backed by
— HTTP client/server built on asyncio transports. Its internals are a good reference implementation of a complex protocol on top of Transport/Protocol.
— PostgreSQL client using Transport/Protocol directly (not streams), achieving the highest throughput of any Python Postgres driver.
— AMQP client (RabbitMQ) built on asyncio Transport/Protocol.
— the spiritual ancestor of asyncio's Transport/Protocol design. Older codebase with different API names but the same architectural concepts; useful for historical context.
as a client, the default context validates the server certificate against system CAs. Never use
in production — this disables all certificate checks and makes the connection trivially interceptable.
Without rate limiting, an attacker can open thousands of connections, consuming file descriptors and memory. Implement a connection counter and reject connections beyond a threshold:
When a client disappears without sending a FIN (due to a crash, network partition, or NAT timeout), the server side can remain in the ESTABLISHED state indefinitely. TCP keepalive detects this:

import socket

# After server creation, set SO_KEEPALIVE on each accepted socket
# via transport.get_extra_info("socket")
sock = transport.get_extra_info("socket")
sock.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_KEEPALIVE, 1)

Better still, implement application-level heartbeats for any long-lived connection. The application heartbeat will detect failures faster than the OS TCP keepalive (which defaults to two hours on many systems).

Unhandled exceptions inside protocol callbacks are logged by asyncio's exception handler but do not propagate to calling code, because there is no awaiting coroutine. Always wrap critical callback logic in try/except and handle failures gracefully — at minimum, log the error and close the transport to prevent a zombie connection.

The Transport/Protocol split separates raw byte transport from application logic, enabling both to evolve independently and making the I/O layer substitutable (plain TCP → SSL, or TCP → UDP) without changing the protocol.
is the critical hook: store the transport here, or nothing will work.
may deliver partial or multiple logical messages; buffering and framing are the protocol's responsibility, not the transport's.
— is not optional for any server that sends substantial data volumes; ignoring it leads to unbounded memory growth.
) is Transport/Protocol in a coroutine-friendly wrapper; understanding the lower layer demystifies the higher one.
with per-packet source addresses; subprocess I/O uses
with per-fd callbacks; SSL is transparent at the transport level.
, connection limits, TCP keepalive, application heartbeats, and executor offload for any CPU-bound work that occurs inside callbacks.
